user_request: Draft a suit for recovery of Rs.5,00,000/- being a hand loan given by the Plaintiff to the Defendant on 10.03.2024 evidenced by a promissory note executed by the Defendant. The loan was repayable on demand or within 6 months. The Plaintiff demanded repayment on 15.09.2024 through legal notice dated 20.09.2024 but the Defendant has failed to repay. Plead under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act for damages and recovery. Claim interest at 12% per annum from date of loan. Include cause of action, limitation, valuation, court fee and verification. Draft suitable for filing before the Civil Judge Senior Division.

facts.summary: Suit for recovery of Rs. 5,00,000/- given as a hand loan to the Defendant on 10.03.2024, evidenced by a promissory note. The loan was repayable on demand or within 6 months. Despite a legal notice dated 20.09.2024 demanding repayment, the Defendant failed to pay.

9. Explanation to the section: Claims for rateable distribution of assets under Section 73 are claims 
enforceable under an attachment. The Explanation to the section was inserted in 1908. A obtains a decree 
against B , and in execution of the decree attaches Rs 7,000 belonging to B in the hands of a railway company. 
B then assigns the said sum in the hands of the railway company to his attorneys for costs due to them subject 
to A s attachment. After the assignment, C , another creditor of B , obtains a decree against B , and in 
execution of his decree, attaches the said sum in the hands of the railway company. Thereafter, the company 
pays the said sum to the Sheriff of Bombay. The assignment by B to his attorneys, though made prior to C s 
attachment, is void as against C s claim, for, C s claim is a claim for rateable distribution of assets (Rs 7,000) 
within the meaning of s 73 and therefore, a claim enforceable under the attachment of A by virtue of the 
explanation to this section. C is, therefore, entitled to be paid in priority to B s attorneys. This is the law under 
the present Code of Civil Procedure, and it is in accordance with the view taken by the Bombay High Court in 
Sorabji v. Govind , 1725 decided under s 276 of the Code of Civil Procedure 1882. The view taken by the other

9. Explanation to the section: Claims for rateable distribution of assets under Section 73 are claims 
enforceable under an attachment. The Explanation to the section was inserted in 1908. A obtains a decree 
against B , and in execution of the decree attaches Rs 7,000 belonging to B in the hands of a railway company. 
B then assigns the said sum in the hands of the railway company to his attorneys for costs due to them subject 
to A s attachment. After the assignment, C , another creditor of B , obtains a decree against B , and in 
execution of his decree, attaches the said sum in the hands of the railway company. Thereafter, the company 
pays the said sum to the Sheriff of Bombay. The assignment by B to his attorneys, though made prior to C s 
attachment, is void as against C s claim, for, C s claim is a claim for rateable distribution of assets (Rs 7,000) 
within the meaning of s 73 and therefore, a claim enforceable under the attachment of A by virtue of the 
explanation to this section. C is, therefore, entitled to be paid in priority to B s attorneys. This is the law under

sections.body:
SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

sections.heading: BETWEEN
sections.instruction: Draft the parties section listing plaintiff and defendant(s) with full particulars: name, age, occupation, and address. Use sui juris averment for competency. If any detail is missing, use the appropriate {{PLACEHOLDER}}.

sections.heading: JURISDICTION
sections.instruction: Draft the jurisdiction section establishing territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction. For territorial jurisdiction, state SPECIFIC facts: where the Defendant resides or carries on business, where payment was made, where the agreement was executed, or where the cause of action arose — with concrete place references. For pecuniary jurisdiction, state the claim amount and that it falls within this court's monetary limits.

sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
sections.instruction: Draft the facts section as numbered paragraphs in chronological narrative form. Each paragraph must tell a story — not state conclusions. Cover: (1) How the parties came to know each other and the nature of their relationship, (2) What was agreed — the specific terms of the transaction/agreement, (3) What obligations each party undertook and the timeline for performance, (4) How payment was made — specific amount, mode, date, (5) What exactly constituted the defendant's failure — describe the specific default with detail, (6) What happened after the default — communications, demands, legal notice and response. Reference documents as 'Annexure [LABEL]'. Do NOT state legal conclusions in this section — keep it purely factual.

sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
sections.instruction: Draft the legal basis section in paragraph form. For each statutory provision: (1) State what the provision says — the legal rule it creates, (2) Explain what factual conditions trigger its application, (3) Show how the facts of this case satisfy those conditions. If applicable, include an alternative plea (e.g., Section 73 ICA as alternative to Section 65 ICA) using 'In the alternative and without prejudice to the above...' framing. Include defensive pleading: pre-emptively neutralize likely defense arguments (e.g., no part performance was rendered, no forfeiture clause exists, no adjustment made, no counter-claim exists). Cite ONLY provisions from the verified list.

must_include.description: At least one statutory section cited
Legal consequence explained (not just section named)

sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
sections.instruction: Draft the cause of action section in PARAGRAPH prose (not numbered sub-headings). Must cover: (1) ORIGIN — when and how the right to sue first arose, anchored to the default/failure date, (2) FURTHER ACCRUAL — when the cause of action further arose (e.g., failure to comply with legal notice), (3) CONTINUITY — that the cause of action is continuing because the defendant continues to wrongfully retain the money. The cause must be anchored to the failure/default date, NOT the notice date. Write in flowing paragraph form — avoid mechanical sub-headings like '(a) underlying obligation'.

sections.heading: LIMITATION
sections.instruction: Draft the limitation section stating: (1) the applicable Article of the Limitation Act, 1963 Schedule (from enrichment or use placeholder), (2) the limitation period, (3) the starting date of limitation (must be anchored to cause_of_action_date/default_date, NOT notice_date), (4) that the suit is within time. If limitation article is not available in enrichment, use {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} placeholder.

sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
sections.instruction: Draft the valuation and court fee section stating: (1) suit valuation equals the claim amount, (2) court fee calculated per applicable state court fees act. Use court_fee context from web search if available, otherwise use {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} placeholder.

sections.heading: INTEREST
sections.instruction: Draft the interest section with a 3-part structure: (1) Pre-suit interest — from default date to filing date with rate and justification, (2) Pendente lite interest — during pendency of suit under Section 34 CPC with rate, (3) Future interest — post-decree until realization with rate. Justify the interest rate: state why the claimed rate is appropriate (commercial transaction, wrongful retention, prevailing bank rate, contractual understanding). Cite Section 34 of CPC for pendente lite interest.

sections.heading: PRAYER
sections.instruction: Draft the prayer section with at least 6 specific sub-prayers as lettered items (a, b, c, ...). Each prayer must state exact amounts or rate placeholders — never vague. Must include: (a) decree for principal amount with Rs. figure, (b) pre-suit interest with rate and date range, (c) pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC with rate, (d) future interest post-decree with rate until realization, (e) costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs for sending legal notice, (f) any other relief the court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances.

sections.heading: LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON
sections.instruction: Generate a numbered list of documents the plaintiff relies upon, mapping to the annexure scheme. For each document: Serial No., Description, Annexure Label. Only list documents mentioned in evidence. If evidence is empty, list standard expected documents with {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}} placeholders.

sections.body:

filled_sections.text:
SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

filled_sections.heading: BETWEEN
filled_sections.text: The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, is sui juris and competent to sue and be sued in this action. The Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, is sui juris and competent to be sued. The parties are bound by a contract evidenced by Annexure A (Agreement/Contract). The Plaintiff contends that a sum is due and payable by the Defendant to the Plaintiff under the contract, as evidenced by Annexure B (Payment proof). In furtherance of the claim, the Plaintiff served a legal notice to the Defendant as evidenced by Annexure C; the Defendant's reply, if any, is contained in Annexure D; the Plaintiff also relies on Annexure E (Acknowledgment/Admission) and Annexure F (Additional supporting documents) in support of the claim. The Plaintiff seeks recovery of the amount due along with interest and costs as permitted by law.

filled_sections.heading: JURISDICTION
FACTS OF THE CASE
filled_sections.text:
1. The Plaintiff and the Defendant were acquainted prior to the transaction, and the Defendant approached the Plaintiff seeking short-term liquidity. On {{DATE}} the Plaintiff advanced Rs 5,00,000 to the Defendant, and this loan was documented by a promissory note executed by the Defendant in favor of the Plaintiff. The promissory note records that the loan was repayable on demand or within six months and that interest would accrue at 12% per annum on the principal. Annexure A: Agreement/Contract document.

2. The terms of the loan were set out in the promissory note signed on {{DATE}}, wherein the Defendant promised to repay the principal sum of Rs 5,00,000 together with interest at 12% per annum either on demand or by no later than six months from the date of disbursement, with interest calculated on the reducing balance. Annexure A.

3. By the same agreement, the Defendant undertook the obligation to repay the loan amount and accrued interest by {{DATE}} or on demand, as the Plaintiff might require. The Plaintiff, in turn, provided the funds on {{DATE}} and retained the right to demand payment as the terms permitted. Annexure A.

4. On {{DATE}} the Plaintiff disbursed Rs 5,00,000 to the Defendant in accordance with the promissory note, which amount constitutes the principal of the hand loan. The promissory note also provides that interest would accrue at 12% per annum from the date of disbursement until repayment. Annexure A.

5. On {{DATE}} the Plaintiff issued a formal demand for repayment of the loan amount and accrued interest. Thereafter, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated {{DATE}} demanding full repayment within a specified period. The Defendant did not make any payment in response to either the demand or the notice. Annexure C.

6. After the breach and in light of the nonpayment, the Plaintiff prepared and filed the suit on {{FILING_DATE}} seeking recovery of the outstanding principal of Rs 5,00,000 together with interest at 12% per annum from {{DATE}} until payment, and costs. The promissory note (Annexure A) and the legal notice (Annexure C) constitute the principal documentary evidence supporting the claim. Annexure A, Annexure C.

filled_sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
filled_sections.text:
Legal Basis and Grounds

Section 73 Indian Contract Act, 1872 empowers an aggrieved party to claim compensation in money for breach of contract, which in the present case arises on the Defendant’s failure to repay the loan advanced by the Plaintiff on 10.03.2024, evidenced by the promissory note (Annexure A). The provision also contains a proviso that “No compensation shall be awarded under this section unless the plaintiff has claimed such compensation in his plaint,” and it further provides that where such compensation has not been claimed, the court may permit amendment of the plaint at any stage on just terms, which in the Plaintiff’s plaint is satisfied by claiming principal and interest and by pleading failure_of_consideration as part of the breach. The legal rule created by this provision is that damages for breach of contract are recoverable, but only to the extent claimed in the plaint, and the court may allow amendment if needed to ensure a just remedy; the trigger is a breach of contract entailing non-performance of a promised return, such as non-repayment under a promissory note. In the present case, the Defendant undertook to repay Rs. 5,00,000 on demand or within six months under the loan arrangement evidenced by Annexure A, and the Plaintiff subsequently demanded repayment on 15.09.2024 (Annexure C is the relevant legal notice of demand dated 20.09.2024), but the Defendant has not repaid. Consequently, the Plaintiff pleads failure_of_consideration to the extent that the consideration advanced on 10.03.2024 has not been returned, and the Plaintiff seeks restitution of the principal sum of Rs. 5,00,000 along with interest at 12% per annum from the due date until payment, together with costs. The factual basis for this claim is the promissory note (Annexure A) and the legal notice (Annexure C) which together establish the contract, its breach, and the resulting entitlement to damages; the remedy sought is restitution of the loan amount along with interest, with the quantum stated in the plaint and supported by the promissory note and demand notices. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, the Plaintiff pleads damages under Section 73 as the exclusive remedy for breach of contract, including the contractual interest at 12% per annum, and the court is empowered to award such damages upon proving the breach and the extent of loss, provided the compensation is claimed in the plaint as mandated by the proviso; the Plaintiff has claimed such compensation in this plaint and will rely on Annexures A, B, and C to support this claim, and if necessary, the plaint may be amended consistent with the court’s leave under the proviso. Defensive pleading is made to neutralize anticipated defenses: there is clear documentation of the loan and its terms (Annexure A), there is a proper notice of demand (Annexure C), and there is no pleaded basis for part performance by the Defendant to negate the breach; there is no forfeiture clause invoked by the Defendant, and no counter-claim or set-off has been raised in the Defendant’s response; accordingly, the relief sought is for restitution of Rs. 5,00,000 with interest, and for such further relief as the Court deems fit under Section 73. Limitation for such recovery is governed by Article 19 of the Limitation Act, which provides a three-year period for money payable for money lent, with accrual at the time the loan is made; the loan having been advanced on 10.03.2024 makes the suit timely, as suit filing would occur within three years of accrual. Annexures A, B, C, and D (where relevant) are relied upon to prove the loan, payment proof if any, and legal notice; the factual basis and documentary support are summarized in the Annexure scheme.

Under Article 19 of the Limitation Act, three years is the period for actions on money lent, and accrual occurs at the time the loan is made; the Plaintiff submits that the present suit is within the limitation period since the loan was advanced on 10.03.2024, and the action is filed within three years from that date, taking into account the date of default and the demand dated 15.09.2024 and the legal notice dated 20.09.2024. This establishes timely enforcement of the Plaintiff’s rights under the contract and the promissory note; accordingly, the claim is not barred by limitation. The remedy of restitution arises from the breach of contract as evidenced by Annexure A, which constitutes the basis of the Plaintiff’s right to recover the principal amount of Rs. 5,00,000 and interest at 12% per annum, along with any costs and such other relief as the Court may grant under the law.

In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, the Plaintiff may seek further relief or adjust the quantum as dictated by the Court’s assessment of actual damages and the applicable contractual rate, and the court’s exercise of discretion under Section 73 to award reasonable compensation for breach, subject to the proviso requiring that compensation be claimed in the plaint. The Plaintiff’s pleadings and the attached documents, including Annexures A and C, provide a solid basis for restitution and any consequential damages arising from the breach, and the Plaintiff reserves the right to seek any further relief as the Court sees just. The anticipated defenses—such as lack of part performance, absence of a forfeiture clause, failure to claim compensation in the plaint, or lack of a counter-claim or set-off—are addressed: the Plaintiff has established the loan, its terms, the breach by non-payment, and proper demand; consequently, those defenses are anticipated and negated by the documentary evidence, the promissory note, and the demand notices.

filled_sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
filled_sections.text: The cause of action for the present suit first arose on {{DATE}} when the Defendant failed to repay Rs. 5,00,000/- under the promissory note dated 10.03.2024, the loan having been advanced on that day and payable on demand or within six months, thereby vesting in the Plaintiff a right to sue for recovery of principal and interest as per the instrument evidenced by the promissory note. The cause of action further arose on 15.09.2024 when the Plaintiff demanded repayment of the full amount and again on 20.09.2024 when the Plaintiff issued a legal notice demanding such repayment, and the Defendant neither paid nor complied with the demand; these subsequent demands reinforce the Plaintiff’s claim but do not alter the date on which the right to sue first accrued. The cause of action is a continuing one because the Defendant continues to wrongfully retain the Plaintiff's sum of Rs. 5,00,000/- and the interest thereon at 12% per annum without any lawful justification, and the Plaintiff’s claim remains unpaid as of the date of filing and shall continue until actual realization.

filled_sections.heading: LIMITATION
filled_sections.text: The present suit is governed by Article 19 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which prescribes a period of three years for suits for money lent. The Plaintiff advanced Rs. 5,00,000 to the Defendant on 10.03.2024 under a promissory note, and the cause of action for non repayment arose on 15.09.2024 when the Defendant defaulted and a demand was raised. The period of limitation therefore runs from 15.09.2024, and would expire on {{DATE}}. The Plaintiff submits that the suit is within time, having been instituted within the three-year period from the date the cause of action accrued, in accordance with Article 19 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963.

filled_sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
filled_sections.text: On {{DATE_OF_FILING}}, the Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, filed this suit against the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, before the {{COURT_NAME}} at {{COURT_LOCATION}} seeking recovery of the principal amount of Rs. 500,000. On the same date, the Plaintiff states that the suit valuation equals Rs. 500,000, which is the claim amount. The court fee payable on the plaint is {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}, calculated in accordance with the applicable state court fees act.

filled_sections.heading: INTEREST
filled_sections.text:
The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, seeks recovery against the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, on account of a contract details of which appear in the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}. Pursuant to that agreement, the Plaintiff paid Rs. 500,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} in contemplation of performance by the Defendant by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}. The Defendant failed to repay/perform by {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, and the Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} which received no satisfactory response; the reply to the notice was received on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}. Consequently, the Suit was filed on {{FILING_DATE}}. The present section contemplates three facets of interest: pre-suit interest, pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC, and future interest post decree until realization, on the principal sum of Rs. 500,000 at the agreed rate of 12.0% per annum.

Pre-suit interest: The Plaintiff claims pre-suit interest at the rate of 12.0% per annum on the principal amount from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}. The legal basis rests on the commercial character of the transaction and the Defendant’s wrongful retention of funds, which deprived the Plaintiff of the use of its money from the date of default, and hence 12.0% per annum is a commercially reasonable mercantile rate that aligns with prevailing market practice during the relevant period and with the contractual understanding between the parties. In substance, the Defendant’s failure to repay by {{DEFAULT_DATE}} caused loss of use of Rs. 500,000, which the Court should compensate at a rate reflecting ordinary commercial expectations.

Pendente lite interest: The Plaintiff seeks pendente lite interest on the same principal amount at 12.0% per annum from {{FILING_DATE}} to {{DECREE_DATE}} under Section 34 CPC. Section 34 CPC authorizes the Court to award interest on money due during the pendency of litigation at such rate as the Court may deem fit, reflecting the circumstances of the case. The rate of 12.0% per annum is justified because the underlying commercial transaction involved money owed and the Defendant unlawfully retained funds, depriving the Plaintiff of the use of Rs. 500,000 during the suit. The rate is consistent with prevailing bank lending rates for short-term credit and with the contractual understanding between the parties, providing just compensation for the Plaintiff’s use of funds during the pendency of the proceedings.

Future interest: The Plaintiff prays for future interest on the decretal amount at 12.0% per annum from the date of decree {{DECREE_DATE}} until realization on {{REALIZATION_DATE}}. The legal basis is the general principle that post-decree interest ensures full restitution for money due and aligns with commercial expectations and past practice in similar money-claims, particularly where the Defendant has wrongfully retained funds. The rate mirrors the agreed 12.0% per annum and serves to deter delay in realization, consistent with contractual understanding between the parties.

filled_sections.heading: PRAYER
filled_sections.text:
(a) Pass a decree directing the Defendant to pay to the Plaintiff a sum of Rs. 5,00,000/- (Rupees Five Lakh Only) being the principal amount advanced under the promissory note dated 10.03.2024.

(b) Direct that the Defendant pay pre-suit interest at the rate of 12% per annum on the aforesaid principal sum of Rs. 5,00,000 from 15.09.2024 to {{FILING_DATE}}.

(c) Award pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC at the rate of {{PENDENTE_LITE_RATE}}% per annum on the principal amount from the date of institution of the suit, i.e., {{FILING_DATE}}, till the date of decree.

(d) Direct that after the decree the Defendant shall pay future interest on the decretal amount at the rate of {{POST_DECREE_INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree till realization.

(e) Award costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs incurred for sending the legal notice dated 20.09.2024 and postage charges, amounting to Rs. {{PRE_LITIGATION_COSTS}}/-, and grant such other costs as the Court may deem fit and proper.

(f) Grant such other relief the Court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

CAUSE OF ACTION

The cause of action for the present suit first arose on {{DATE}} when the Defendant failed to repay Rs. 5,00,000/- under the promissory note dated 10.03.2024, the loan having been advanced on that day and payable on demand or within six months, thereby vesting in the Plaintiff a right to sue for recovery of principal and interest as per the instrument evidenced by the promissory note. The cause of action further arose on 15.09.2024 when the Plaintiff demanded repayment of the full amount and again on 20.09.2024 when the Plaintiff issued a legal notice demanding such repayment, and the Defendant neither paid nor complied with the demand; these subsequent demands reinforce the Plaintiff’s claim but do not alter the date on which the right to sue first accrued. The cause of action is a continuing one because the Defendant continues to wrongfully retain the Plaintiff's sum of Rs. 5,00,000/- and the interest thereon at 12% per annum without any lawful justification, and the Plaintiff’s claim remains unpaid as of the date of filing and shall continue until actual realization.


LIMITATION

The present suit is governed by Article 19 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which prescribes a period of three years for suits for money lent. The Plaintiff advanced Rs. 5,00,000 to the Defendant on 10.03.2024 under a promissory note, and the cause of action for non repayment arose on 15.09.2024 when the Defendant defaulted and a demand was raised. The period of limitation therefore runs from 15.09.2024, and would expire on {{DATE}}. The Plaintiff submits that the suit is within time, having been instituted within the three-year period from the date the cause of action accrued, in accordance with Article 19 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

On {{DATE_OF_FILING}}, the Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, filed this suit against the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, before the {{COURT_NAME}} at {{COURT_PLACE}} seeking recovery of the principal amount of Rs. 500,000. On the same date, the Plaintiff states that the suit valuation equals Rs. 500,000, which is the claim amount. The court fee payable on the plaint is {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}, calculated in accordance with the applicable state court fees act.


INTEREST

The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, seeks recovery against the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, on account of a contract details of which appear in the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}. Pursuant to that agreement, the Plaintiff paid Rs. 500,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} in contemplation of performance by the Defendant by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}. The Defendant failed to repay/perform by {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, and the Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} which received no satisfactory response; the reply to the notice was received on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}. Consequently, the Suit was filed on {{FILING_DATE}}. The present section contemplates three facets of interest: pre-suit interest, pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC, and future interest post decree until realization, on the principal sum of Rs. 500,000 at the agreed rate of 12.0% per annum.

Pre-suit interest: The Plaintiff claims pre-suit interest at the rate of 12.0% per annum on the principal amount from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}. The legal basis rests on the commercial character of the transaction and the Defendant’s wrongful retention of funds, which deprived the Plaintiff of the use of its money from the date of default, and hence 12.0% per annum is a commercially reasonable mercantile rate that aligns with prevailing market practice during the relevant period and with the contractual understanding between the parties. In substance, the Defendant’s failure to repay by {{DEFAULT_DATE}} caused loss of use of Rs. 500,000, which the Court should compensate at a rate reflecting ordinary commercial expectations.

Pendente lite interest: The Plaintiff seeks pendente lite interest on the same principal amount at 12.0% per annum from {{FILING_DATE}} to {{DECREE_DATE}} under Section 34 CPC. Section 34 CPC authorizes the Court to award interest on money due during the pendency of litigation at such rate as the Court may deem fit, reflecting the circumstances of the case. The rate of 12.0% per annum is justified because the underlying commercial transaction involved money owed and the Defendant unlawfully retained funds, depriving the Plaintiff of the use of Rs. 500,000 during the suit. The rate is consistent with prevailing bank lending rates for short-term credit and with the contractual understanding between the parties, providing just compensation for the Plaintiff’s use of funds during the pendency of the proceedings.

Future interest: The Plaintiff prays for future interest on the decretal amount at 12.0% per annum from the date of decree {{DECREE_DATE}} until realization on {{REALIZATION_DATE}}. The legal basis is the general principle that post-decree interest ensures full restitution for money due and aligns with commercial expectations and past practice in similar money-claims, particularly where the Defendant has wrongfully retained funds. The rate mirrors the agreed 12.0% per annum and serves to deter delay in realization, consistent with contractual understanding between the parties.


PRAYER

(a) Pass a decree directing the Defendant to pay to the Plaintiff a sum of Rs. 5,00,000/- (Rupees Five Lakh Only) being the principal amount advanced under the promissory note dated 10.03.2024.

(b) Direct that the Defendant pay pre-suit interest at the rate of 12% per annum on the aforesaid principal sum of Rs. 5,00,000 from 15.09.2024 to {{FILING_DATE}}.

(c) Award pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC at the rate of {{PENDENTE_LITE_RATE}}% per annum on the principal amount from the date of institution of the suit, i.e., {{FILING_DATE}}, till the date of decree.

(d) Direct that after the decree the Defendant shall pay future interest on the decretal amount at the rate of {{POST_DECREE_INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree till realization.

(e) Award costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs incurred for sending the legal notice dated 20.09.2024 and postage charges, amounting to Rs. {{PRE_LITIGATION_COSTS}}/-, and grant such other costs as the Court may deem fit and proper.

(f) Grant such other relief the Court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

8. Serial No.: 2; Description: Legal Notice issued by the Plaintiff to the Defendant demanding repayment, dated 20.09.2024, served at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}; Annexure Label: Annexure C.

No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

BETWEEN:

{{PLAINTIFF_NAME}},
S/o./D/o. {{PLAINTIFF_PARENT_NAME}},
Aged about {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years,
Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}.                                  ...Plaintiff

{{DEFENDANT_NAME}},
S/o./D/o. {{DEFENDANT_PARENT_NAME}},
Aged about {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years,
Occupation: {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}.                                  ...Defendant


PLAINT

JURISDICTION

1. Territorial Jurisdiction: The Defendant resides at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}} within the local limits of this Hon’ble Court. The hand loan of Rs. 5,00,000/- was paid/handed over at {{LOAN_DISBURSEMENT_LOCATION}}, and the promissory note was executed by the Defendant at {{EXECUTION_PLACE}} on 10.03.2024. Hence, this Hon’ble Court has territorial jurisdiction.

2. Pecuniary Jurisdiction: The suit claim is for recovery of Rs. 5,00,000/- (Rupees Five Lakh Only) together with interest, which is within the pecuniary jurisdiction of this Hon’ble Court.


FACTS OF THE CASE

3. The Plaintiff and the Defendant are known to each other. On 10.03.2024, the Defendant approached the Plaintiff seeking a hand loan for his/her personal needs/urgencies. Believing the Defendant’s representations, the Plaintiff advanced a sum of Rs. 5,00,000/- (Rupees Five Lakh Only) to the Defendant on 10.03.2024.

4. On the same day i.e., 10.03.2024, the Defendant executed a promissory note in favour of the Plaintiff acknowledging receipt of Rs. 5,00,000/- and promising to repay the said amount with interest at 12% per annum. The promissory note stipulates that the loan is repayable on demand or within six (6) months from 10.03.2024. A copy of the promissory note is filed herewith as Annexure A.

5. Six months from the date of loan expired on 10.09.2024. The Defendant failed and neglected to repay the loan amount within the said period.

6. Thereafter, on 15.09.2024, the Plaintiff demanded repayment from the Defendant, but the Defendant did not repay the amount.

7. The Plaintiff then caused a legal notice dated 20.09.2024 to be issued to the Defendant demanding repayment of the loan amount together with interest at 12% per annum. The legal notice is filed as Annexure B and the postal/serving proof is filed as Annexure C.

8. Despite receipt/service of the legal notice, the Defendant has neither replied with any valid justification nor repaid any part of the loan amount. The Defendant is thus in breach of his/her obligation under the promissory note and is liable to pay the Plaintiff the principal sum of Rs. 5,00,000/- along with interest.


LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

9. The Defendant executed the promissory note dated 10.03.2024 (Annexure A) and thereby undertook a contractual obligation to repay the loan amount on demand or within six months, together with interest at 12% per annum.

10. The Defendant’s failure to repay the loan amount within the agreed time and even after demand/notice constitutes breach of contract.

11. The Plaintiff pleads Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 as the basis to claim compensation/damages arising from the Defendant’s breach. The loss suffered by the Plaintiff flows naturally from the breach, namely the non-repayment/wrongful retention of the principal sum of Rs. 5,00,000/- and the agreed interest thereon. Accordingly, the Plaintiff is entitled to a decree for recovery of the principal and interest, together with costs.


CAUSE OF ACTION

12. The cause of action for the suit arose on 10.03.2024 when the Defendant borrowed Rs. 5,00,000/- from the Plaintiff and executed the promissory note. The cause of action further arose on 10.09.2024 when the Defendant failed to repay within six months as agreed. The cause of action further arose on 15.09.2024 when the Plaintiff demanded repayment and again on 20.09.2024 when the Plaintiff issued a legal notice, and continues from day to day so long as the Defendant fails to repay the outstanding amount.


LIMITATION

13. The suit is filed within the period of limitation prescribed for recovery actions of this nature. The amount became due, at the latest, on 10.09.2024 upon expiry of six months from 10.03.2024 and non-payment by the Defendant, and the present suit has been instituted within three years therefrom. (If a specific article citation is required, the Plaintiff seeks leave to cite the applicable provision as {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} upon verification.)


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

14. The suit is valued for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee as follows:

(a) Principal claim: Rs. 5,00,000/-.

(b) Pre-suit interest at 12% per annum from 10.03.2024 to {{DATE_OF_FILING}}: Rs. {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_AMOUNT}}/-.

(c) Total suit valuation (principal + pre-suit interest): Rs. {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUATION}}/-.

15. The requisite court fee on the above valuation is paid as per the applicable Court Fees Act and Rules. Court fee paid/payable: Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}/-.


INTEREST

16. The Plaintiff claims:

(a) Pre-suit interest @ 12% per annum on Rs. 5,00,000/- from 10.03.2024 to {{DATE_OF_FILING}} (amounting to Rs. {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_AMOUNT}}/-);

(b) Pendente lite interest under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 on the principal sum of Rs. 5,00,000/- at 12% per annum (or such rate as this Hon’ble Court deems fit) from the date of filing of the suit till the date of decree; and

(c) Future interest under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 on the decretal amount at 12% per annum (or such rate as this Hon’ble Court deems fit) from the date of decree till realization.


PRAYER

Wherefore, the Plaintiff respectfully prays that this Hon’ble Court be pleased to:

(a) Pass a decree directing the Defendant to pay the Plaintiff the principal sum of Rs. 5,00,000/- (Rupees Five Lakh Only) being the hand loan advanced under the promissory note dated 10.03.2024;

(b) Award pre-suit interest at 12% per annum on Rs. 5,00,000/- from 10.03.2024 to {{DATE_OF_FILING}}, amounting to Rs. {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_AMOUNT}}/-;

(c) Award pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC on the principal sum of Rs. 5,00,000/- at 12% per annum (or such rate as this Hon’ble Court deems fit) from the date of institution of the suit till the date of decree;

(d) Award future interest under Section 34 CPC on the decretal amount at 12% per annum (or such rate as this Hon’ble Court deems fit) from the date of decree till realization;

(e) Award costs of the suit; and

(f) Grant such other relief(s) as this Hon’ble Court deems fit in the facts and circumstances of the case.


LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON

1. Annexure A: Promissory Note dated 10.03.2024 executed by the Defendant in favour of the Plaintiff.
2. Annexure B: Legal Notice dated 20.09.2024 issued on behalf of the Plaintiff to the Defendant demanding repayment.
3. Annexure C: Postal receipt / tracking report / acknowledgment evidencing dispatch/service of the legal notice.


VERIFICATION

blocking_issues.issue: Facts section is not aligned to the USER_REQUEST and is drafted with incorrect/irrelevant placeholders and assertions (e.g., repeated {{DATE}} instead of the known loan/notice dates; references to an 'Agreement/Contract', 'payment proof', 'reducing balance', etc.). blocking_issues.location: BETWEEN (opening factual paragraph) and FACTS OF THE CASE (Paras 1–6)

blocking_issues.issue: Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 is pleaded with incorrect content (including a quoted/proviso-style requirement that 'No compensation shall be awarded unless claimed in plaint' and amendment language). This misstates the law and risks legal error if filed as-is.
blocking_issues.fix: Preserve the user-requested citation to 'Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872' but remove the incorrect quoted/proviso language; replace with a correct, case-relevant pleading that Section 73 permits compensation/damages for breach, and link it to the defendant’s non-repayment of the promissory note/hand loan.
blocking_issues.location: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS (entire Section 73 discussion)

blocking_issues.issue: Interest claim is inconsistent with the USER_REQUEST. blocking_issues.issue: Cause of action / limitation is inconsistently and incorrectly anchored to procedural demand/default dates, and the draft contradicts itself on accrual (stating accrual at time of loan in one place and from 15.09.2024 in another). For a loan repayable within 6 months, the due date (10.09.2024) is a key accrual anchor; the demand/notice are subsequent steps.
blocking_issues.fix: Anchor the primary accrual at the due date upon expiry of 6 months from 10.03.2024 (i.e., 10.09.2024) and plead subsequent accrual on demand/notice as continuing/refusal events; ensure limitation paragraph follows the same accrual anchor and does not treat notice/demand as the limitation start if the underlying obligation became enforceable earlier.
blocking_issues.location: CAUSE OF ACTION paragraph and LIMITATION paragraph

blocking_issues.issue: Statutory reference to 'Article 19 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963' is not supported by provided RAG_CHUNKS and was not user-requested; the specific article number may be incorrect for a promissory-note/demand loan, creating a legal risk if filed as-is.
blocking_issues.fix: Remove the specific article-number citation and instead plead limitation in a provision-neutral manner (e.g., 'within three years from the date when the amount became due/right to sue accrued'); if a specific article is to be cited, replace with {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} placeholder pending verification.
blocking_issues.location: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS (limitation sentence) and LIMITATION section

blocking_issues.issue: Document/annexure reference label mismatch: the body refers to Annexure A as 'Agreement/Contract', and also refers to Annexures B, D, E, F, but the List of Documents contains only Annexure A and Annexure C; additionally, Annexure labels are used inconsistently for the promissory note and notice.
blocking_issues.fix: Adopt a single annexure scheme and use it consistently in body and List of Documents. Remove references to non-existent annexures, or add them to the list only if actually relied upon. For this case, align Annexure A = Promissory Note; Annexure B = Legal Notice dated 20.09.2024; Annexure C = Postal receipt/track report/acknowledgment (if available).
blocking_issues.location: Opening paragraph under BETWEEN; FACTS; LEGAL BASIS; and LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON

non_blocking_issues.issue: Legal analysis is mixed into the factual narrative in multiple places (e.g., detailed legal-rule exposition inside facts/grounds).
non_blocking_issues.fix: Keep FACTS as pure chronology; move doctrinal explanations and anticipated defenses to a separate LEGAL BASIS section (already present) with concise linkage to pleaded facts.
non_blocking_issues.location: FACTS OF THE CASE and LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

non_blocking_issues.issue: Prayer could be made more arithmetically specific by stating the pre-suit interest amount up to the filing date and the total suit valuation (principal + pre-suit interest), subject to calculation.
non_blocking_issues.fix: Add {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_AMOUNT}} and {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUATION}} placeholders and reflect them consistently in valuation/court fee and prayer.

non_blocking_issues.issue: Territorial jurisdiction paragraph can be tightened by stating clearly where the promissory note was executed and where payment was made (bank/branch/place), to strengthen territorial links.
non_blocking_issues.fix: Specify execution place and payment place (or keep {{LOAN_DISBURSEMENT_LOCATION}}/{{EXECUTION_PLACE}} placeholders) without adding unnecessary commentary.

unsupported_statements.statement: “The parties are bound by a contract evidenced by Annexure A (Agreement/Contract). … Annexure B (Payment proof). … Defendant's reply, if any, is contained in Annexure D; … Annexure E (Acknowledgment/Admission) and Annexure F (Additional supporting documents) …”
unsupported_statements.reason: USER_REQUEST provides only a promissory note and a legal notice; no payment-proof annexure, reply, acknowledgment/admission, or additional supporting documents are provided/pleaded with facts. The draft asserts documents without factual foundation.
No such term is stated in USER_REQUEST; typical promissory-note/hand-loan pleadings do not assume 'reducing balance' without an agreed schedule.

unsupported_statements.statement: “pleading failure_of_consideration as part of the breach.”
unsupported_statements.reason: This is an irrelevant/unsupported legal characterization for a hand loan evidenced by a promissory note; the pleaded cause is non-repayment/breach, not failure of consideration.
